item.title: Nintendo is raising Switch 2 prices around the world
item.content:encoded:
Chris Kerr,

Senior Editor, News,GameDeveloper.com

May 8, 2026

2 Min Read

Nintendo is increasing the price of Switch 2 hardware in key markets such as Japan, the United States, Canada, and Europe.

Less than a year after the Switch 2 debuted in June 2025, the company said it has been forced to revise the price of hardware and others services including Nintendo Switch Online in response to "various changes in market conditions."

The news comes a few months after Nintendo president Shuntaro Furukawa said an ongoing memory shortage related to rampant investment in AI data centres could "put pressure on profitability."

In a note to investors, Nintendo explained it will be implementing the following hardware price increases in Japan on May 25, 2026, and across the United States, Canada, and Europe on September 1, 2026.

Japan

  • Nintendo Switch 2 (Japanese-Language System): Revised Price ¥59,980 // Current Price ¥49,980

  • Nintendo Switch (OLED Model): Revised Price ¥47,980 // Current Price ¥37,980

  • Nintendo Switch: Revised Price ¥43,980 // ¥32,978

  • Nintendo Switch Lite: Revised Price ¥29,980 // ¥21,978

Related:GameStop wants eBay, Nintendo confirms Switch 2 price hike, and union boss says EA buyout is a national security risk - Patch Notes #51

United States

  • Nintendo Switch 2: Revised Price $499.99 // Current Price $449.99

Canada

  • Nintendo Switch 2: Revised Price $679.99 // Current Price $629.99

Europe 

  • Nintendo Switch 2: Revised Price €499.99 // Current Price €469.99

The price of the following Nintendo Switch Online subscriptions is also being raised in Japan: 

Nintendo Switch Online 

  • 1 month: Revised Price ¥400 // Current Price ¥306 

  • 3 months: Revised Price ¥1,000 // Current Price ¥815

  • 12 months: Revised Price ¥3,000 // Current Price ¥2,400

  • 12 months (Family Membership): Revised Price ¥5,800 // Current Price ¥4,500

Nintendo Switch Online + Expansion Pack

  • Individual Membership (12 months): Revised Price ¥5,900 // Current Price ¥4,900

  • Family Membership (12 months): Revised Price ¥9,900 // Current Price ¥8,900

Nintendo added that Switch Online price revisions are also planned in South Korea. It also issued an apology after confirming the changes. 

"We sincerely apologize for the impact these price revisions may have on our customers and other stakeholders, and we deeply appreciate your understanding," said the company. 

Other major console manufacturers including Microsoft and Sony have raised the price of video game hardware in recent months, citing 'changes in the macroeconomic environment' and 'continued pressures in the global economic landscape,' respectively.

About the Author

Senior Editor, News, GameDeveloper.com

Game Developer news editor Chris Kerr is an award-winning reporter with over a decade of experience in the game industry. His byline has appeared in notable print and digital publications including Edge, Stuff, Wireframe, International Business Times, and PocketGamer.biz. Throughout his career, Chris has covered major industry events including GDC, PAX Australia, Gamescom, Paris Games Week, and Develop Brighton.

item.title: Tomodachi Life: Living The Dream Sales Are Off To A Flying Start
item.content:encoded:
Would you like to talk about... FINANCIAL DATA?

Tomodachi Life: Living the Dream may have launched too late for Nintendo to give us a precise figure on its sales numbers in the latest financial report (which collects data up to the end of March 2026), though it has assured us that things are off to a flying start.

According to the report, Living the Dream has sold over 3.8 million units globally so far — and that's in just its first two weeks on the market!

Of course, those who remember the 3DS game might not find this quick start all that surprising. The last Tomodachi Life became the system's 10th best-selling game, with its 6.73 million units putting it above the likes of A Link Between Worlds and the Ocarina / Majora remakes.

Still, 3.8 million in two weeks isn't bad going at all, particularly considering Nintendo's attempts to restrict its online sharing. Never doubt a bit of word of mouth, eh?

Based on how Living the Dream is knocking on four million sales after its first two weeks and the Switch 1's massive install base, we imagine the latest title will surpass its predecessor's numbers in no time. Heck, it seems to be staying strong in both the UK and Japanese charts for the time being.

If you're yet to get on the Tomodachi Life train and you're wondering what it's all about, you'll find our full thoughts in our review below.

Have you contributed to Living the Dream's opening sales? Let us know in the comments.

[source nintendo.co.jp]

Jim came to Nintendo Life in 2022 and, despite his insistence that The Minish Cap is the best Zelda game and his unwavering love for the Star Wars prequels (yes, really), he has continued to write news and features on the site ever since.
